items.q: What is the difference between a shift in demand and a movement along the demand curve?
items.correct: A movement along the curve is caused by a change in price; a shift of the whole curve is caused by changes in other factors (income, tastes, substitute prices).
items.misconception: A change in price shifts the demand curve.
items.random_wrong: Demand always falls when supply increases.
items.why: This is the single most common error in introductory economics. Price changes cause movements ALONG the curve; the curve itself shifts only when non-price factors change.

items.q: What is the difference between CPI and RPI as inflation measures?
items.correct: Both measure price changes via a basket of goods, but RPI includes housing costs (mortgage interest) and uses an arithmetic mean; CPI excludes housing costs and uses a geometric mean — CPI is the official UK target measure.
items.misconception: CPI and RPI always give the same inflation figure.
items.random_wrong: CPI measures consumer prices and RPI measures retail prices — that's the only difference.
items.why: RPI usually runs higher than CPI due to formula and coverage differences — this matters hugely for pensions, rail fares and benefits. Students treat the two as identical and miss the policy implications of which is used.

items.correct: Features of the tax and benefit system that automatically cushion the economy without new legislation — e.g. unemployment benefits rise in slumps; tax revenues fall as incomes fall, supporting demand.
items.misconception: Automatic stabilisers are interest rate changes by the central bank.
items.random_wrong: Automatic stabilisers are special government emergency funds.
items.why: Stabilisers are fiscal (tax/benefit) and automatic (no decision needed) — distinct from monetary policy or discretionary stimulus. Students confuse them with central bank action or with one-off bailout funds.
It's Kahneman and Tversky. “Simon” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's Simon. “Friedman” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Kahneman and Tversky publish prospect theory?
items.why: Exact answer: 1979 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
